name: swin

members.name: SwinConfig
members.signature: SwinConfig(image_size: int = 224, patch_size: int = 4, in_channels: int = 3, num_classes: int = 1000, embed_dim: int = 96, depths: tuple[int, ...] = (2, 2, 6, 2), num_heads: tuple[int, ...] = (3, 6, 12, 24), window_size: int = 7, mlp_ratio: float = 4.0, dropout: float = 0.0, attention_dropout: float = 0.0, drop_path_rate: float = 0.0)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(image_size: int = 224, patch_size: int = 4, in_channels: int = 3, num_classes: int = 1000, embed_dim: int = 96, depths: tuple[int, ...] = (2, 2, 6, 2), num_heads: tuple[int, ...] = (3, 6, 12, 24), window_size: int = 7, mlp_ratio: float = 4.0, dropout: float = 0.0, attention_dropout: float = 0.0, drop_path_rate: float = 0.0)

members.summary: Configuration dataclass for every Swin Transformer variant.
members.extended:
`SwinConfig` is an immutable container that fully specifies the
architecture of a Swin Transformer.  It is consumed by both
`SwinTransformer` (backbone) and
`SwinTransformerForImageClassification` (classifier).  All
canonical variants described in [Liu et al. (2021)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.14030) — Tiny / Small /
Base / Large — can be expressed by choosing different per-stage
depths, channel widths, and head counts.

The backbone is a four-stage pyramid: the stem patch embedding maps
$(H, W, 3) \to (H/p, W/p, C)$, then each stage applies a stack
of pairs of Swin blocks (window + shifted-window attention) and a
patch-merging downsampler that halves the spatial size while
doubling the channel width, giving the canonical
$C, 2C, 4C, 8C$ pyramid.

parameters.name: image_size
parameters.description:
Spatial side length (in pixels) of the square input image.
Defaults to `224`.  Must be divisible by `patch_size`.

parameters.name: patch_size
parameters.description:
Side length of the non-overlapping patches extracted by the stem
convolution.  Defaults to `4`.

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of input image channels.  Defaults to `3` (RGB).

parameters.name: num_classes
parameters.description:
Number of output classes for the classification head.  Defaults
to `1000` (ImageNet-1k).

parameters.name: embed_dim
parameters.description:
Channel width $C$ after patch embedding (stage-1 dim).
Each subsequent stage doubles, so the final stage has width
$8C$.  Defaults to `96` (Swin-T).

parameters.name: depths
parameters.description:
Number of Swin blocks in each of the four stages.  Defaults to
`(2, 2, 6, 2)` (Swin-T).

parameters.name: num_heads
parameters.description:
Number of attention heads in each stage.  Defaults to
`(3, 6, 12, 24)` (Swin-T).

parameters.name: window_size
parameters.description:
Side length $M$ of the local attention window inside each
Swin block.  Defaults to `7`.

parameters.name: mlp_ratio
parameters.description: Expansion ratio of the MLP block.  Defaults to `4.0`.

parameters.name: dropout
parameters.description:
Dropout probability inside the MLP feed-forward layer and after
the patch embedding.  Defaults to `0.0`.

parameters.name: attention_dropout
parameters.description:
Dropout probability on post-softmax attention weights.  Defaults
to `0.0`.

parameters.name: drop_path_rate
parameters.description:
Stochastic-depth maximum rate; linearly scheduled across all
blocks of the trunk (Liu et al., 2021, Appendix A).  The paper
uses `0.2` for Swin-T and `0.3` for Swin-S/B/L.  Defaults to
`0.0`.

examples:
Build a Swin-Tiny configuration for CIFAR-100 (100 classes):
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import SwinConfig
>>> cfg = SwinConfig(num_classes=100)
>>> cfg.embed_dim, cfg.depths, cfg.num_heads
(96, (2, 2, 6, 2), (3, 6, 12, 24))
Override the stem patch size for a higher-resolution variant:
>>> cfg = SwinConfig(patch_size=8, image_size=384)
>>> cfg.image_size // cfg.patch_size  # tokens per side
48
notes:
The canonical variants registered as factory functions in
`lucid.models.vision.swin` are (see Table 7 of the paper):

============= ========== ================== ===================
Variant       embed_dim  depths             num_heads
============= ========== ================== ===================
Swin-T        96         (2, 2, 6, 2)       (3, 6, 12, 24)
Swin-S        96         (2, 2, 18, 2)      (3, 6, 12, 24)
Swin-B        128        (2, 2, 18, 2)      (4, 8, 16, 32)
Swin-L        192        (2, 2, 18, 2)      (6, 12, 24, 48)
============= ========== ================== ===================

Reference: Ze Liu *et al.*, *"Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision
Transformer using Shifted Windows"*, ICCV 2021,
[arXiv:2103.14030](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.14030).

attributes.name: model_type
attributes.description: Constant string `"swin"` used by the model registry.

members.name: SwinTransformer
members.extended:
A Swin Transformer first splits the input image into non-overlapping
$4 \times 4$ patches via a strided convolution and then
progresses through four hierarchical stages.  Each stage stacks
pairs of Swin blocks alternating between *window* attention (W-MSA)
and *shifted-window* attention (SW-MSA), with patch-merging
downsamplers between stages that halve spatial resolution and
double channel width:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{z}^{l} &= \mathrm{W\text{-}MSA}(\mathrm{LN}(z^{l-1})) + z^{l-1}, \\
z^{l} &= \mathrm{MLP}(\mathrm{LN}(\hat{z}^{l})) + \hat{z}^{l}, \\
\hat{z}^{l+1} &= \mathrm{SW\text{-}MSA}(\mathrm{LN}(z^{l})) + z^{l}, \\
z^{l+1} &= \mathrm{MLP}(\mathrm{LN}(\hat{z}^{l+1})) + \hat{z}^{l+1}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Window attention partitions the feature map into non-overlapping
$M \times M$ windows and runs self-attention inside each,
making the per-image cost linear in $HW$.  The shifted variant
cycles each window by $\lfloor M/2 \rfloor$ pixels so that
information flows between neighbouring windows across pairs of
blocks.  A learnable *relative position bias* is added inside the
softmax of every window attention.

`forward_features` returns a global-average-pooled
$(B, 8C)$ feature where $C = \texttt{embed\_dim}$.  Use
this backbone when you need features rather than logits — e.g.
transfer learning, dense prediction, or contrastive pretraining.
For end-to-end image classification, use
`SwinTransformerForImageClassification` instead.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen dataclass specifying `image_size`, `patch_size`,
`embed_dim`, `depths`, `num_heads`, `window_size`,
`mlp_ratio`, `dropout`, `attention_dropout`,
`drop_path_rate`, and `in_channels`.  See `SwinConfig`.

examples:
Build a Swin-Tiny backbone and run a forward pass:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import SwinTransformer, SwinConfig
>>> model = SwinTransformer(SwinConfig())
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> feat = model.forward_features(x)
>>> feat.shape                       # (B, 8 * embed_dim)
(1, 768)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape      # (B, 1, 8 * embed_dim)
(1, 1, 768)
notes:
Reference: Ze Liu *et al.*, *"Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision
Transformer using Shifted Windows"*, ICCV 2021,
[arXiv:2103.14030](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.14030).

attributes.name: stages
attributes.description:
Four hierarchical `_SwinStage` modules, each containing a
stack of Swin blocks and (for the first three) a patch-merging
downsampler.

attributes.name: norm
attributes.description:
Final LayerNorm applied to the channel-last feature map before
global pooling.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
$1 \times 1$ adaptive average pool used to produce the
final $(B, 8C)$ feature.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Four-stage feature description with per-stage channel counts
$(C, 2C, 4C, 8C)$ and spatial reductions
$(p, 2p, 4p, 8p)$ relative to the input.

members.name: SwinTransformerForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: Swin Transformer with a linear classification head (Liu et al., 2021).
members.extended:
Wraps the same hierarchical trunk as `SwinTransformer` (patch
embedding → four stages of window / shifted-window attention →
LayerNorm) and adds a global average pool plus a single
`nn.Linear` classification head that maps the
$(B, 8C)$ feature to `config.num_classes` logits:

$$
\text{logits} = W_{\text{head}}\,
    \mathrm{GAP}(\mathrm{LN}(z^{L})) + b_{\text{head}},
\qquad W_{\text{head}} \in \mathbb{R}^{C_{\text{out}} \times 8C}.
$$

Pass `labels` to `forward` to compute the cross-entropy
loss in the same pass.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture specification.  Must set `num_classes` to the
desired number of output categories (default 1000 for ImageNet).
See `SwinConfig`.

examples:
End-to-end inference with the default Swin-T classifier:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import (
...     SwinConfig, SwinTransformerForImageClassification,
... )
>>> model = SwinTransformerForImageClassification(SwinConfig())
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Reference: Ze Liu *et al.*, *"Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision
Transformer using Shifted Windows"*, ICCV 2021,
[arXiv:2103.14030](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.14030).  Swin-T /
S / B / L reach **81.3 / 83.0 / 83.5 / 86.4 % top-1 on ImageNet-1k**
(Table 1 of the paper, 224x224 input).

attributes.name: stages
attributes.description: Four hierarchical Swin stages with patch-merging downsamplers.

attributes.name: norm
attributes.description: Final LayerNorm applied to the channel-last feature map.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description: $1 \times 1$ adaptive average pool over spatial dims.

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description:
Final linear projection of width `(num_classes, 8 * embed_dim)`
built via `ClassificationHeadMixin._build_classifier`.

members.name: swin_tiny
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *Swin-T* configuration: `embed_dim=96`,
`depths=(2, 2, 6, 2)`, `num_heads=(3, 6, 12, 24)`,
`window_size=7`.  With the default 224x224 input the trunk
produces a $(B, 768)$ feature after the final stage's
$7 \times 7$ map is globally pooled.  Approximately
**28M parameters**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k pretrained weights when available
in the model zoo.  Defaults to `False` (random initialization).

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides applied on top of the canonical Swin-T config.
Common examples: `image_size=384`, `in_channels=1`,
`window_size=12`.  Each override must match a field of
`SwinConfig`.

returns.description:
A `SwinTransformer` backbone returning a flat
$(B, 768)$ feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import swin_tiny
>>> model = swin_tiny()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> feat = model.forward_features(x)
>>> feat.shape
(1, 768)
notes:
Swin-T reaches **81.3% top-1 / 95.5% top-5 on ImageNet-1k**
(Liu et al., 2021, Table 1).  See
[arXiv:2103.14030](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.14030).

members.name: swin_tiny_cls
members.signature: swin_tiny_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: SwinTinyWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.extended:
Combines the `swin_tiny` backbone with a global average pool
+ single `nn.Linear` classification head.  Default output is
`num_classes=1000` (ImageNet-1k).  Approximately **28M parameters**
for the 1000-class head.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`SwinTinyWeights.IMAGENET1K_V1`);
a tag string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive
with `weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides on top of the canonical Swin-T config.
Commonly used: `num_classes` to switch label space,
`image_size` to fine-tune at a different resolution.

returns.description:
Classifier returning `ImageClassificationOutput` whose
`logits` has shape `(B, num_classes)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import swin_tiny_cls
>>> model = swin_tiny_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Swin-T reaches **81.3% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** (Liu et al., 2021).
Pretrained weights are converted from torchvision's
`Swin_T_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1` and hosted under
`lucid-dl/swin-tiny`.

members.name: swin_small
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *Swin-S* configuration: `embed_dim=96`,
`depths=(2, 2, 18, 2)`, `num_heads=(3, 6, 12, 24)`.  Same width
as Swin-T but with 18 blocks in stage 3 instead of 6, roughly
doubling the compute.  Approximately **50M parameters**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k pretrained weights when available.
Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical Swin-S config.

returns.description:
A `SwinTransformer` backbone returning a flat
$(B, 768)$ feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import swin_small
>>> model = swin_small()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 768)
notes:
Swin-S reaches **83.0% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** (Liu et al., 2021,
Table 1).

members.name: swin_small_cls
members.signature: swin_small_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: SwinSmallWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.extended:
Combines the `swin_small` backbone (`embed_dim=96`,
`depths=(2, 2, 18, 2)`) with a global average pool plus a linear
classification head.  ~50M parameters.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`SwinSmallWeights.IMAGENET1K_V1`);
a tag string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive
with `weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical Swin-S config.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import swin_small_cls
>>> model = swin_small_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Swin-S reaches **83.0% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** (Liu et al., 2021,
Table 1).  Pretrained weights are converted from torchvision's
`Swin_S_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1` and hosted under
`lucid-dl/swin-small`.

members.name: swin_base
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *Swin-B* configuration: `embed_dim=128`,
`depths=(2, 2, 18, 2)`, `num_heads=(4, 8, 16, 32)`.  Same depth
as Swin-S but ~33% wider per stage; final feature dimension is
$8 \cdot 128 = 1024$.  Approximately **88M parameters**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-1k or ImageNet-22k pretrained
weights when available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical Swin-B config.

returns.description:
A `SwinTransformer` backbone returning a flat
$(B, 1024)$ feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import swin_base
>>> model = swin_base()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 1024)
notes:
Swin-B reaches **83.5% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** (224x224) and
**86.4% top-1** when fine-tuned at 384x384 after ImageNet-22k
pretraining (Liu et al., 2021, Tables 1 and 2).

members.name: swin_base_cls
members.signature: swin_base_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: SwinBaseWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.extended:
Combines the `swin_base` backbone (`embed_dim=128`,
`depths=(2, 2, 18, 2)`) with a global average pool plus a linear
classification head.  Final feature width is $8 \cdot 128 = 1024$.  ~88M parameters.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`SwinBaseWeights.IMAGENET1K_V1`);
a tag string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive
with `weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical Swin-B config.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import swin_base_cls
>>> model = swin_base_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Swin-B reaches **83.5% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** (224x224) and
**86.4% top-1** at 384x384 after ImageNet-22k pretraining (Liu
et al., 2021, Tables 1 and 2).  The shipped checkpoint is converted
from torchvision's `Swin_B_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1` and hosted under
`lucid-dl/swin-base`.

members.name: swin_large
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *Swin-L* configuration: `embed_dim=192`,
`depths=(2, 2, 18, 2)`, `num_heads=(6, 12, 24, 48)`.  Final
feature dimension is $8 \cdot 192 = 1536$.  Approximately
**197M parameters**.  Pretrained variants are typically released
only with ImageNet-22k pretraining due to the model's capacity.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-22k pretrained weights when
available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical Swin-L config.

returns.description:
A `SwinTransformer` backbone returning a flat
$(B, 1536)$ feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import swin_large
>>> model = swin_large()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 1536)
notes:
Swin-L reaches **87.3% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** (384x384) after
ImageNet-22k pretraining (Liu et al., 2021, Table 2).

members.name: swin_large_cls
members.signature: swin_large_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: SwinLargeWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.extended:
Combines the `swin_large` backbone (`embed_dim=192`,
`depths=(2, 2, 18, 2)`) with a global average pool plus a linear
classification head.  Final feature width is $8 \cdot 192 = 1536$.  ~197M parameters.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag
(`SwinLargeWeights.MS_IN22K_FT_IN1K`); a tag string →
that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive with `weights`
(which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical Swin-L config.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.swin import swin_large_cls
>>> model = swin_large_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Swin-L reaches **87.3% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 384x384
fine-tune resolution after ImageNet-22k pretraining (Liu
et al., 2021, Table 2).  The shipped checkpoint is the 224x224
ImageNet-22k → ImageNet-1k finetune (86.3% top-1), converted from
timm's `swin_large_patch4_window7_224.ms_in22k_ft_in1k` and
hosted under `lucid-dl/swin-large`.

members.name: SwinBaseWeights
members.extended:
Ships a single ImageNet-1k checkpoint (`IMAGENET1K_V1`)
converted from the reference-framework `Swin_B_Weights` and
re-hosted under `huggingface.co/lucid-dl/swin-base`.

members.name: SwinLargeWeights
members.extended:
Ships the original Microsoft ImageNet-22k → ImageNet-1k finetune
(`MS_IN22K_FT_IN1K`), re-hosted via timm
(`swin_large_patch4_window7_224.ms_in22k_ft_in1k`) under
`huggingface.co/lucid-dl/swin-large`.  The paper (Liu et al.,
2021, Table 2) reports 86.3% top-1 for this checkpoint at 224×224.

members.name: SwinSmallWeights
members.extended:
Ships a single ImageNet-1k checkpoint (`IMAGENET1K_V1`)
converted from the reference-framework `Swin_S_Weights` and
re-hosted under `huggingface.co/lucid-dl/swin-small`.

members.name: SwinTinyWeights
members.extended:
Ships a single ImageNet-1k checkpoint (`IMAGENET1K_V1`)
converted from the reference-framework `Swin_T_Weights` and
re-hosted under `huggingface.co/lucid-dl/swin-tiny`.

summary: Swin Transformer family — Liu et al., 2021.
citation: Liu, Ze, et al. "Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision, 2021, pp. 10012-10022.
theory:
The Swin Transformer reintroduces the *hierarchical* feature
pyramid of classical CNNs into a pure-transformer backbone, making
it a general-purpose vision model that scales linearly with image
resolution rather than quadratically (as plain ViT does).  The
image is first split into non-overlapping $4 \times 4$
patches and projected to $C$ channels.  Four stages then
follow, each halving the spatial resolution via a *patch merging*
layer that concatenates $2 \times 2$ neighbours and projects
$4C \to 2C$, doubling the channel width — giving the
canonical $C, 2C, 4C, 8C$ pyramid.

Within each stage the tokens flow through pairs of Swin blocks.
The first block computes self-attention inside non-overlapping
*windows* of size $M \times M$ (default $M = 7$), and
the second performs *shifted-window* (SW-MSA) attention where the
grid is cyclically shifted by $\lfloor M/2 \rfloor$.  Because
each window is processed independently, the attention cost per
image is $\mathcal{O}(M^2 H W)$ instead of
$\mathcal{O}((HW)^2)$, but the shift introduces
cross-window connections so the receptive field still grows with
depth.  A learnable *relative position bias*
$B \in \mathbb{R}^{M^2 \times M^2}$ is added inside softmax:



$$
\mathrm{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(
    \frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d}} + B
\right) V.
$$

The four canonical variants (Tiny / Small / Base / Large) scale
$C$ and per-stage block counts from Table 7 of the paper,
trading parameters for accuracy while reusing the same
shifted-window mechanism end to end.
